Correlation Analysis: Marketing Dataset

v0.3 — Simplified + Data Cleaning

2026-05-17

1. Dataset Description

This dataset contains customer records from a retail company, including demographic information, household details, and purchasing behavior across different product categories.

Source: Kaggle — Customer Personality Analysis

Dataset dimensions:
Number of customers 2240
Number of variables 30

Description of key variables used in this analysis:

Variable Description
Income Customer’s yearly household income (USD)
TotalSpend Total spending across all product categories in the last 2 years (derived)
Age Customer’s age (derived from Year_Birth)
Education Customer’s highest education level
Kidhome Number of children in the household
Teenhome Number of teenagers in the household

2. Data Cleaning — Removing Erroneous Records

Before any analysis, we inspect the data for values that are technically valid numbers but clearly incorrect in real-world terms. Two problems were found.

Issue Reason Decision
Income = $666,666 The second highest income is $162,397 — a $500,000 gap makes this a data entry error Removed
Year_Birth = 1893 (age 132) Would make this customer 132 years old — biologically impossible Removed
Year_Birth = 1899 (age 126) Would make this customer 126 years old — biologically impossible Removed
Year_Birth = 1900 (age 125) Would make this customer 125 years old — biologically impossible Removed

4 rows were removed (from 2240 to 2236 customers).

Why remove rather than correct? We do not know what the true values should have been. For the income entry of $666,666, we could theoretically impute a replacement — but since we are already imputing other missing income values, replacing an obviously wrong value with yet another estimate would layer uncertainty on top of uncertainty. For the birth years, there is no plausible correction. Removing these 4 rows has negligible impact on a dataset of 2236 observations.

3. Research Question

Selected variables: income_noNA (annual household income, with missing values imputed) and TotalSpend (total spending)

Research Question: Is there an association between a customer’s annual household income and their total spending on products over the last two years?

4. Variable Types and Missing Values

Both variables are numerical (continuous). Before analysis, we check for missing values.

Variable Type Missing Count Missing (%)
Income Numeric 24 1.1%
TotalSpend Numeric 0 0%

Income has 24 missing values. Instead of deleting these rows, we estimate the missing incomes using a Random Forest model in the next section.

5. Income Imputation

Rather than dropping the 24 customers with missing income, we predict their likely income using a Random Forest — a machine learning algorithm that builds many decision trees and averages their predictions.

Predictors used to estimate missing income:

Predictor Rationale
Education Education level is a strong predictor of earning capacity
Age Income tends to peak mid-career
TotalSpend Spending behaviour reflects available disposable income
Kidhome Household size affects income needs
Teenhome Household size affects income needs
NumCatalogPurchases Catalogue buyers tend to be higher-income customers
NumStorePurchases Store visit frequency reflects purchasing power

Model performance on the held-out test set:

Metric Value
RMSE (Root Mean Squared Error) $11,856
R² (Variance explained) 0.715

The model explains 71.5% of the variance in income (R² = 0.715). The average prediction error is approximately $11,856 — acceptable given that income in this dataset ranges from $1,730 to $162,397 (a spread of over $160,000).

Distribution of original vs imputed income values:

The imputed values follow a similar distribution to the observed ones, which gives us confidence that the model is producing realistic estimates. We now use income_noNA (which contains the original values where available, and imputed values where not) in all subsequent analyses.

6. Descriptive Statistics

Variable n Mean Std. Dev. Median Min Max
income_noNA (USD) 2,236 51,933 21,464 51,342 1,730 162,397
TotalSpend (USD) 2,236 606 602 396 5 2,525

The average customer income is $51,933 (SD = $21,464, Median = $51,342), with values ranging from $1,730 to $162,397. Total spending is considerably more skewed: the mean is $606 but the median is only $396, indicating that a small number of high-spending customers pull the average upward. Spending ranges from $5 to $2,525.

7. Scatterplot

The scatterplot shows a clear upward trend: customers with higher incomes generally spend more. The cloud of points widens at higher income levels, suggesting that variance in spending increases with income (heteroscedasticity) — this is one reason we also test non-parametric and log-transformed correlations below.

8. Correlation Analysis

8.1 Assumption Check

Pearson correlation assumes a linear relationship and is sensitive to outliers. To check whether these assumptions hold, we compare the smooth LOESS curve against the straight regression line, and inspect whether residuals fan out.

  • Left plot: If the red LOESS curve closely follows the dashed straight line, the relationship is linear. A curved LOESS suggests non-linearity.
  • Right plot: If the spread of points grows from left to right (a funnel/fan shape), heteroscedasticity is present — meaning Pearson’s significance test becomes less reliable.

8.2 Three Correlation Coefficients

Because the assumption checks may reveal issues with raw Pearson, we calculate three measures side by side:

Method What it measures Assumption
Pearson Linear association on raw values Linearity + normality
Log-Pearson Linear association after log-transforming both variables Log-linear relationship
Spearman Monotonic association based on ranks No linearity needed, robust to outliers

8.3 Results

Method Coefficient (r / ρ) p-value 95% CI
Pearson (raw scale) 0.793 <2e-16 [0.777, 0.808]
Log-Pearson (log scale) 0.759 <2e-16 [0.741, 0.776]
Spearman (rank-based) 0.853 <2e-16 —

Interpretation:

All three methods reveal a strong, statistically significant positive association between income and total spending (all p < 2e-16).

  • Spearman (ρ = 0.853) is the highest of the three. This tells us that the rank ordering of customers by income very closely matches their rank ordering by spending — a strong monotonic relationship. Spearman is not affected by outliers or the non-normal tails we observed in the Q-Q plot, which makes it the most reliable measure here.
  • Pearson (r = 0.793) is notably lower than Spearman (gap of 0.06). This gap is explained by the Cook’s Distance and Q-Q results in §9: a small number of influential observations and non-normal residual tails are pulling the Pearson coefficient downward relative to the true strength of the association.
  • Log-Pearson (r = 0.759) is the lowest of the three, meaning the log transformation does not improve the fit here. The relationship is not primarily log-linear — the rank-based pattern (captured by Spearman) is a better description of the data structure.

Conclusion on method choice: Spearman (ρ = 0.853) is the primary measure for this analysis. The Cook’s Distance plot showed a small number of high-leverage observations, and the Q-Q plot confirmed non-normal tails — both conditions where Spearman outperforms Pearson. Raw Pearson (r = 0.793) is reported for completeness and remains consistent in direction and significance, but should be interpreted cautiously.

9. Outliers and Influential Observations

Variable Outliers (IQR method)
income_noNA 7
TotalSpend 3

The Cook’s Distance plot identifies observations that have an unusually large influence on the regression line. Points above the dashed threshold (4/n = 0.0018) are flagged as influential — there are 122 such observations (5.5% of the dataset). Note that while the graph visually shows only a few tall bars, the threshold value is very small (≈0.0018), so many bars that appear flat also exceed it mathematically. Combined with the non-normal tails in the Q-Q plot, this confirms that Spearman is the appropriate primary measure over Pearson.

10. Correlation Matrix and Heatmap

Since the dataset contains more than four numerical variables, we present a full correlation matrix across the main spending and purchasing variables.

The heatmap reveals that wine and meat spending are most strongly correlated with income, followed by catalog purchases. Recency (days since last purchase) shows little to no correlation with income, suggesting that purchase frequency is not driven by financial capacity alone.

11. Conclusion and Limitations

This analysis examined the association between customers’ annual household income and their total spending over the last two years. All three correlation methods agree on a strong, positive, and statistically significant association (all p < 2e-16). Customers with higher incomes consistently tend to spend more at this retailer.

The Spearman coefficient (ρ = 0.853) is the preferred primary measure. It is the strongest of the three and is robust to the influential observations identified by Cook’s Distance and the non-normal residual tails shown in the Q-Q plot. The Pearson coefficient (r = 0.793) corroborates the finding but is modestly pulled down by a small number of high-leverage customers. The correlation matrix further shows that income is most strongly associated with wine spending (r = 0.69), store purchases (r = 0.64), and catalog purchases (r = 0.63), while Recency (days since last purchase) shows virtually no association with income (r = 0.01–0.02).

Limitations:

  • Missing income values were estimated using a machine learning model rather than observed directly. While the model performs reasonably well (R² = 0.715), imputed values carry uncertainty not present in the original data.
  • Circular dependency: TotalSpend was used as a predictor to impute Income, and is also the outcome variable in the correlation — this may slightly inflate the observed association.
  • Company-specific spending: TotalSpend only captures purchases at this retailer. High-income customers who shop elsewhere will appear as low spenders, weakening the true relationship.
  • Country differences: Customers come from multiple countries with very different income levels and purchasing power. Pooling them without adjustment may distort the results.
  • No causation: A correlation does not tell us that higher income causes higher spending. Other factors — age, education, household size — likely play a role as well.
